news_summary: Innovative technologies harnessing natural resources and sustainable materials are advancing environmental justice and marine conservation efforts.
news_summary_fr: Les technologies innovantes exploitant les ressources naturelles et les matériaux durables font progresser la justice environnementale et les efforts de conservation marine.
news_summary_es: Las tecnologías innovadoras que aprovechan los recursos naturales y los materiales sostenibles están impulsando la justicia medioambiental y los esfuerzos de conservación marina.

articles.title: New engine uses the freezing cold of space to generate power at night
articles.summary: Engineers at UC Davis have built a remarkable device that creates power at night by tapping into something we rarely think about: the vast cold of outer space. Using a special type of Stirling engine, the system links the warmth of the ground to the freezing depths above us, generating mechanical energy simply from the natural temperature difference after sunset.
articles.content:
Engineers at the University of California, Davis have created a device that produces mechanical power at night by taking advantage of the temperature difference between the warmth of the Earth and the extreme cold of outer space. The system could eventually help ventilate greenhouses and other buildings without relying on fuel. The research appears in Science Advances.

The device is based on a Stirling engine, a type of machine designed to convert heat into mechanical motion. Unlike internal combustion engines, which require a large temperature gap to operate efficiently, Stirling engines can run on much smaller differences in heat. Jeremy Munday, professor of electrical and computer engineering at UC Davis and co author of the study, explained that the temperature contrast can be as modest as that between a hot cup of coffee and the surrounding air.

"These engines are very efficient when only small temperature differences exist, whereas other types of engines work better with larger temperature differences and can produce more power," Munday said.

In a typical setup, one side of a Stirling engine is kept warm while the other side is cooled. The temperature contrast drives a piston, which in turn produces mechanical energy.

"If you just set it on the table, it's not going to produce any power on its own because all sides are the same temperature," Munday said.

Using Deep Space as a Cold Reservoir

Usually, a temperature difference is created by heating one side of the engine with a fuel source. Instead of burning fuel, Munday and graduate student researcher Tristan Deppe explored whether the cold side could be linked to something far colder and much more distant: deep space.

"It doesn't actually have to touch space physically, it can just interact radiatively with space," Munday said. On a clear, cool night, heat naturally radiates away from your body toward the sky, which makes your head feel colder. The team aimed to use that same effect.

Their design places a simple Stirling engine (essentially a piston driving a flywheel) on top of a panel that acts as a heat radiating antenna. The entire system sits outdoors at night. The ground provides warmth to one side of the engine, while the panel releases heat upward, effectively connecting the other side to the cold of space.

Nighttime Experiments Show Promising Results

After a year of testing at night, the researchers found that the compact device could generate at least 400 milliwatts of mechanical power per square meter. In demonstrations, the engine directly powered a small fan. It was also connected to a small electric motor to produce electrical current.

These results show that meaningful amounts of energy can be captured from the night sky. According to Munday, the approach works best in regions with low humidity and consistently clear skies. In the future, the technology could help ventilate greenhouses or residential buildings without conventional energy sources.

UC Davis has filed a provisional patent related to the invention.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a novel, sustainable technology that generates power at night by harnessing the temperature difference between the Earth and outer space, offering a clean energy solution with potential broad environmental benefits such as reducing fuel reliance for ventilation in buildings and greenhouses.
articles.personality_title: New engine generates power at night using cold of outer space
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists at the University of California, Davis, have been working on ways to create energy without using fuel. One challenge is making power at night when the sun isn’t shining. They focused on using the natural coldness of outer space as a source of energy.

**What happened** – The engineers built a device based on a Stirling engine, which produces motion from temperature differences. This engine uses the warm ground as one side and the very cold sky as the other. At night, heat moves from the Earth through the engine up to space, creating mechanical energy. After testing for a year, the device produced enough power to run a small fan and generate electricity.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows energy can be made at night without burning fuel or using sunlight. The device works best in places with clear skies and dry air. It could be used to power ventilation in greenhouses or homes, reducing the need for electricity from traditional sources.

**What's next step** – The team has filed a patent to protect their invention. They will likely continue improving the device’s design and look for ways to use it in real buildings. If successful, it could offer a new way to save energy and lower pollution.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Engineers created a Stirling engine that uses the cold of outer space at night to produce clean mechanical power without fuel.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau moteur produit de l'énergie la nuit grâce au froid de l'espace
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des scientifiques de l'Université de Californie à Davis cherchent des moyens de produire de l'énergie sans utiliser de carburant. Un défi est de générer de l'énergie la nuit, quand le soleil ne brille pas. Ils ont alors pensé à utiliser le froid naturel de l'espace comme source d'énergie.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Les ingénieurs ont construit un appareil basé sur un moteur Stirling, qui produit du mouvement grâce à une différence de température. Ce moteur utilise la chaleur du sol d'un côté et le froid très intense du ciel de l'autre. La nuit, la chaleur passe de la Terre à travers le moteur vers l'espace, ce qui crée de l'énergie mécanique. Après un an d'essais, l'appareil a produit assez d'énergie pour faire fonctionner un petit ventilateur et générer de l'électricité.

**Impact** – C'est important car cela montre qu'on peut produire de l'énergie la nuit sans brûler de carburant ni utiliser la lumière du soleil. L'appareil fonctionne mieux dans les endroits avec un ciel clair et un air sec. Il pourrait être utilisé pour ventiler des serres ou des maisons, réduisant ainsi la consommation d'électricité classique.

**Prochaine étape** – L'équipe a déposé un brevet pour protéger leur invention. Ils vont probablement continuer à améliorer le moteur et chercher des applications dans des bâtiments réels. Si cela réussit, ce sera un nouveau moyen d'économiser de l'énergie et de réduire la pollution.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des ingénieurs ont créé un moteur Stirling qui utilise le froid de l'espace la nuit pour produire une énergie mécanique propre sans carburant.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo motor genera energía nocturna usando el frío del espacio exterior
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Científicos de la Universidad de California en Davis buscan formas de producir energía sin usar combustible. Un reto es generar energía por la noche, cuando no hay sol. Pensaron en usar el frío natural del espacio como fuente de energía.

**Qué pasó** – Los ingenieros construyeron un dispositivo basado en un motor Stirling, que produce movimiento gracias a la diferencia de temperatura. Este motor usa el calor de la tierra en un lado y el frío extremo del cielo en el otro. Por la noche, el calor se mueve de la Tierra a través del motor hacia el espacio, creando energía mecánica. Después de un año de pruebas, el dispositivo produjo suficiente energía para hacer funcionar un pequeño ventilador y generar electricidad.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra que se puede producir energía por la noche sin quemar combustible ni usar la luz solar. El dispositivo funciona mejor en lugares con cielos despejados y aire seco. Podría usarse para ventilar invernaderos o casas, reduciendo la necesidad de electricidad tradicional.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo ha registrado una patente para proteger su invento. Probablemente continuarán mejorando el diseño y buscarán aplicaciones en edificios reales. Si tiene éxito, podría ofrecer una nueva forma de ahorrar energía y reducir la contaminación.

**Resumen en una frase** – Ingenieros crearon un motor Stirling que usa el frío del espacio exterior por la noche para producir energía mecánica limpia sin combustible.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a small Stirling engine with a visible piston and flywheel gently turning atop a glowing warm earth surface panel, while above it a dark, starry night sky with subtle radiating lines symbolizing cold space draws heat upward, all rendered in soft natural tones with a calm, hopeful atmosphere.

articles.title: I will continue the fight for environmental justice in Black communities
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 27 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00538-4Nicknamed the father of environmental justice, Robert Bullard argues that researchers in the field have more reason than ever to back up their work with action.
articles.content:
Nicknamed the father of environmental justice, Robert Bullard argues that researchers in the field have more reason than ever to back up their work with action.

Environmental justice leader Robert Bullard speaks to residents in his hometown of Elba in Alabama about flooding in the historic Black Shiloh community there. Credit: Bullard Center for Environmental and Climate Justice

In the 1990s, Robert Bullard championed the then-emerging field of environmental justice. The field embraces the principle that all people and communities have a right to equal protection and equal enforcement of their environmental, health, housing, employment, energy, transportation and civil-rights laws and regulations. His first community-led research project, in the late 1970s, documented that solid-waste landfill sites in Houston, Texas, were more likely to be found in neighbourhoods that are predominantly Black. His data have bolstered lawsuits and policy changes to challenge the placing of landfill sites near predominantly Black schools.

doi: https://doi.org/10.1038/d41586-026-00538-4

This interview has been edited for length and clarity.

Related Articles

Subjects
articles.reasoning: The article highlights Robert Bullard's impactful work in environmental justice, focusing on equal protection for marginalized Black communities against environmental hazards. It emphasizes meaningful, community-led research that has led to policy changes and legal challenges, benefiting society broadly by addressing systemic environmental inequalities.
articles.personality_title: Robert Bullard continues fight for environmental justice in Black communities
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Robert Bullard is known as the father of environmental justice. This field focuses on making sure all communities, especially those often ignored like Black neighborhoods, are treated fairly when it comes to environmental laws and health protections.

**What happened** – Bullard, who started working on this issue in the 1970s, has shown that harmful sites like landfills are often placed near Black neighborhoods, such as in Houston, Texas. His research has helped communities fight back by changing policies and winning legal cases. Recently, he spoke to residents in his hometown of Elba, Alabama, about flooding problems in a historic Black community.

**Impact** – Bullard’s work is important because it shows how unfair environmental decisions hurt certain groups more than others. By collecting clear evidence and involving local people, he has helped make those problems visible and pushed for real solutions. This helps protect health and improve living conditions in places that were overlooked before.

**What's next step** – Bullard believes researchers must now do more than just study problems—they need to take action alongside communities to create change. This means more community-led projects and stronger efforts to enforce fair environmental rules.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Robert Bullard’s decades of research and activism continue to support Black communities in fighting environmental unfairness and pushing for equal protection under the law.
articles.personality_title_fr: Robert Bullard poursuit la lutte pour la justice environnementale dans les communautés noires
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Robert Bullard est reconnu comme le père de la justice environnementale. Ce domaine vise à assurer que toutes les communautés, surtout celles souvent ignorées comme les quartiers noirs, soient traitées équitablement en matière de lois environnementales et de protections sanitaires.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Bullard, qui travaille sur ce sujet depuis les années 1970, a montré que les sites nuisibles comme les décharges sont souvent placés près des quartiers noirs, notamment à Houston, Texas. Ses recherches ont aidé ces communautés à se défendre en modifiant les politiques et en remportant des procès. Récemment, il a parlé aux habitants de sa ville natale, Elba en Alabama, à propos des inondations dans une communauté noire historique.

**Impact** – Le travail de Bullard est important car il révèle comment des décisions environnementales injustes affectent davantage certains groupes. En rassemblant des preuves claires et en impliquant les habitants, il a aidé à rendre ces problèmes visibles et à pousser pour des solutions concrètes. Cela protège la santé et améliore les conditions de vie dans des endroits auparavant négligés.

**Prochaine étape** – Bullard estime que les chercheurs doivent désormais faire plus que simplement étudier les problèmes : ils doivent agir avec les communautés pour créer du changement. Cela signifie plus de projets dirigés par les habitants et des efforts renforcés pour appliquer des règles environnementales justes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les décennies de recherches et d’activisme de Robert Bullard continuent de soutenir les communautés noires dans leur lutte contre l’injustice environnementale et pour une protection égale devant la loi.
articles.personality_title_es: Robert Bullard continúa la lucha por la justicia ambiental en comunidades negras
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Robert Bullard es conocido como el padre de la justicia ambiental. Este campo busca que todas las comunidades, especialmente las que suelen ser ignoradas como los barrios negros, reciban un trato justo en las leyes ambientales y las protecciones de salud.

**Qué pasó** – Bullard, que trabaja en este tema desde los años 70, demostró que sitios dañinos como los vertederos suelen ubicarse cerca de barrios negros, como en Houston, Texas. Sus investigaciones ayudaron a estas comunidades a defenderse cambiando políticas y ganando casos legales. Recientemente, habló con residentes de su ciudad natal, Elba en Alabama, sobre problemas de inundaciones en una comunidad negra histórica.

**Impacto** – El trabajo de Bullard es importante porque muestra cómo decisiones ambientales injustas afectan más a ciertos grupos. Al reunir pruebas claras e involucrar a los locales, ayudó a hacer visibles estos problemas y a impulsar soluciones reales. Esto protege la salud y mejora las condiciones de vida en lugares antes ignorados.

**Próximo paso** – Bullard cree que los investigadores deben hacer más que estudiar problemas; deben actuar junto a las comunidades para generar cambios. Esto implica más proyectos liderados por la comunidad y esfuerzos para hacer cumplir reglas ambientales justas.

**Frase clave** – Las décadas de investigación y activismo de Robert Bullard continúan apoyando a las comunidades negras en su lucha contra la injusticia ambiental y por una protección igualitaria ante la ley.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a resilient Black community depicted as a vibrant neighborhood with colorful homes and children playing safely, surrounded by a protective barrier of glowing, intertwined hands symbolizing environmental justice and solidarity, with subtle imagery of clean water flowing gently nearby and a balanced scale subtly integrated into the landscape to represent fairness and equal protection.

articles.title: Ocean geoengineering trial finds no evidence of harm to marine life
articles.summary: Pouring 65,000 litres of sodium hydroxide into the Gulf of Maine removed up to 10 tonnes of carbon dioxide from the atmosphere without harming wildlife, according to the researchers behind an ocean alkalinity enhancement test
articles.content:
Pouring 65,000 litres of sodium hydroxide into the Gulf of Maine removed up to 10 tonnes of carbon dioxide from the atmosphere without harming wildlife, according to the researchers behind an ocean alkalinity enhancement test

Alkaline sodium hydroxide was dumped into the Gulf of Maine to test its effect on carbon uptake and marine life Daniel Cojanu, Undercurrent Productions, ©Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution

Can we safely remove carbon dioxide from the atmosphere by counteracting ocean acidification? Maybe, suggests a trial in which ships poured 65,000 litres of alkaline sodium hydroxide into the Gulf of Maine off the East Coast of the US in August 2025.

“We’re the first group to do a ship-based alkalinity enhancement experiment,” says Adam Subhas at the Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution in Massachusetts, whose team announced their initial findings at the Ocean Sciences Meeting in Glasgow, UK, on 25 February. “We can definitely say that there was additional CO2 uptake as a result of this experiment.”

Advertisement

Between 2 and 10 tonnes of CO2 was removed from the atmosphere in the following four days, Subhas says, and the team estimates that up to 50 tonnes could be removed altogether. What’s more, no significant effect on marine life was seen.

However, when asked by New Scientist, Subhas acknowledged that the team has yet to estimate the emissions required to manufacture the sodium hydroxide and transport it to the trial site. This means it is unclear whether the trial resulted in a net removal of CO2.

“It’s a really good question,” said Subhas. “That’s going to be a really critical area of research moving forward.”

Free newsletter Sign up to The Earth Edition Unmissable news about our planet, delivered straight to your inbox each month. Sign up to newsletter

The oceans store 40 times as much carbon as the atmosphere and have soaked up more than a quarter of the excess CO2 we have been pumping into the atmosphere. This extra CO2 reacts with water to form carbonic acid, meaning that the oceans are becoming more acidic.

Ocean acidification could have a major impact on many marine organisms, for instance, by dissolving their carbonate shells. It also reduces the ability of the seas to take up more CO2.

Researchers are exploring a number of methods to counteract ocean acidification, including adding magnesium hydroxide to wastewater that goes into the ocean, adding ground-up olivine to coasts and pumping seawater through land-based treatment plants. Some companies are already selling carbon credits based on alkalinity enhancement.

“This is something that the private sector is moving forward with right now,” says Subhas, which is why there is a need for non-commercial trials like the one his team did.

Because of the controversial nature of these kinds of trials, the team started by engaging with local people, particularly in the fishing community, says team member Kristin Kleisner of the Environmental Defense Fund, a non-profit organisation based in New York. “Two-way dialogue is really critical,” she says.

The trial itself involved three ships and was monitored in several different ways, ranging from satellites to floating sensors to ocean gliders that zigzag up and down. The sodium hydroxide was mixed with trace quantities of a dye called rhodamine, to help accurately track its dispersal.

The team measured the concentrations of microbes, plankton, fish larvae and lobster larvae, and also the level of photosynthetic activity, says Rachel Davitt at Rutgers University in New Jersey. “There was no significant impact of our field trial on the biological community,” she says.

The extra carbon taken up by the ocean as a result of the increased alkalinity is turned into bicarbonate ions, or dissolved baking soda, Subhas says. “We expect that this carbon is locked away for tens of thousands of years. It’s one of the most durable forms of carbon removal.”

The nature of the process means that CO2 is removed and stored in a single step, Subhas says. This is an advantage over some other approaches, where CO2 is first removed from the atmosphere and then has to be permanently stored in some form.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a successful ocean alkalinity enhancement trial that removed significant CO2 from the atmosphere without harming marine life, indicating a promising method to counteract ocean acidification and climate change. The trial has broad environmental significance and includes detailed scientific context and monitoring, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Ocean trial safely removes carbon dioxide without harming sea life
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The ocean absorbs a lot of carbon dioxide (CO2) from the air, but this causes ocean acidification. Acidic oceans can harm marine animals like shellfish and reduce the ocean’s ability to take in more CO2. Scientists are testing ways to reduce acidification and capture CO2 safely in the ocean.

**What happened** – In August 2025, researchers poured 65,000 litres of sodium hydroxide, a chemical that makes water less acidic, into the Gulf of Maine off the US East Coast. This experiment was led by the Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution. They used three ships and tracked the chemical’s spread with sensors and satellites. Over four days, the ocean absorbed between 2 and 10 tonnes of CO2 thanks to the added alkalinity, with no harm found to marine life like fish, plankton, or lobster larvae.

**Impact** – This trial is the first ship-based test of ocean alkalinity enhancement, showing it can increase the ocean’s ability to soak up CO2 without damaging sea creatures. The CO2 is converted into a stable form, similar to dissolved baking soda, which can stay locked away for thousands of years. This method removes and stores CO2 in one step, which is simpler than other techniques that need separate removal and storage processes.

**What's next step** – Scientists still need to calculate how much CO2 is released to make and transport the sodium hydroxide to see if the whole process results in net CO2 removal. More research and trials will explore this, and the team plans to continue working with local communities, especially fishermen, to ensure safety and transparency.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A carefully monitored ocean experiment successfully removed carbon dioxide without harming marine life, offering a promising way to help reduce ocean acidification and capture carbon safely.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une expérience océanique élimine le dioxyde de carbone sans nuire à la vie marine
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’océan absorbe beaucoup de dioxyde de carbone (CO2) de l’air, mais cela rend l’eau plus acide. Cette acidification peut nuire aux animaux marins comme les coquillages et réduit la capacité de l’océan à absorber davantage de CO2. Les scientifiques testent des méthodes pour réduire cette acidité et capturer le CO2 en toute sécurité.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En août 2025, des chercheurs ont versé 65 000 litres d’hydroxyde de sodium, un produit chimique qui réduit l’acidité de l’eau, dans le golfe du Maine, sur la côte Est des États-Unis. Cette expérience a été menée par l’Institut océanographique Woods Hole. Trois navires ont suivi la dispersion du produit grâce à des capteurs et satellites. En quatre jours, l’océan a absorbé entre 2 et 10 tonnes de CO2 grâce à cette augmentation d’alcalinité, sans effet nocif sur la vie marine comme les poissons, le plancton ou les larves de homard.

**Impact** – C’est la première expérience en mer utilisant cette méthode d’augmentation de l’alcalinité. Elle montre qu’on peut augmenter la capacité de l’océan à absorber le CO2 sans abîmer les êtres vivants marins. Le CO2 est transformé en une forme stable, semblable à du bicarbonate dissous, qui peut rester stockée pendant des milliers d’années. Cette méthode capture et stocke le CO2 en une seule étape, ce qui est plus simple que d’autres techniques.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs doivent encore calculer les émissions liées à la fabrication et au transport de l’hydroxyde de sodium pour savoir si l’ensemble du procédé retire bien plus de CO2 qu’il n’en rejette. D’autres études et essais sont prévus, avec une collaboration continue avec les communautés locales, notamment les pêcheurs, pour garantir la sécurité et la transparence.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une expérience océanique bien suivie a permis d’éliminer du dioxyde de carbone sans nuire à la vie marine, offrant une méthode prometteuse pour réduire l’acidification des océans et capturer le carbone en toute sécurité.
articles.personality_title_es: Prueba oceánica elimina dióxido de carbono sin dañar la vida marina
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El océano absorbe mucho dióxido de carbono (CO2) del aire, pero esto hace que el agua se vuelva más ácida. La acidificación puede dañar animales marinos como los moluscos y reduce la capacidad del océano para absorber más CO2. Los científicos están probando formas de reducir esta acidez y capturar CO2 de manera segura.

**Qué pasó** – En agosto de 2025, investigadores vertieron 65,000 litros de hidróxido de sodio, una sustancia que reduce la acidez del agua, en el Golfo de Maine, en la costa este de Estados Unidos. El equipo del Instituto Oceanográfico Woods Hole lideró el experimento. Usaron tres barcos y monitorearon la dispersión con sensores y satélites. En cuatro días, el océano absorbió entre 2 y 10 toneladas de CO2 gracias a esta alcalinidad añadida, sin afectar a la vida marina como peces, plancton o larvas de langosta.

**Impacto** – Es la primera prueba en barco de mejora de alcalinidad oceánica, demostrando que puede aumentar la capacidad del océano para absorber CO2 sin dañar a los seres marinos. El CO2 se transforma en una forma estable, parecida a bicarbonato disuelto, que puede permanecer almacenada durante miles de años. Este método elimina y almacena el CO2 en un solo paso, lo que es más sencillo que otras técnicas.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos aún deben calcular cuántas emisiones se generan al fabricar y transportar el hidróxido de sodio para saber si el proceso es realmente una reducción neta de CO2. Se planean más investigaciones y pruebas, y el equipo continuará trabajando con las comunidades locales, especialmente pescadores, para asegurar la seguridad y transparencia.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un experimento oceánico bien controlado eliminó dióxido de carbono sin dañar la vida marina, ofreciendo una forma prometedora de reducir la acidificación oceánica y capturar carbono de manera segura.
articles.image_prompt: A serene seascape painting showing three research ships gently releasing a soft glowing blue-green mist into calm ocean waters near the Gulf of Maine, with subtle swirling patterns symbolizing dissolved carbon dioxide transforming into harmless bicarbonate, while diverse marine life like plankton and lobster larvae appear peacefully thriving beneath the surface, all rendered in warm, natural tones with a harmonious, hopeful atmosphere.

articles.title: Can surfboards, handbags and coffins be made from mushrooms?
articles.summary: Can mushrooms replace leather and plastic? Thanks to mycelium derived from fungi, companies can now make 100 percent biodegradable products such as shoes and furniture that look exactly like leather. Major brands are already trying to make the shift. Our France 2 colleagues report.
articles.content:
Can surfboards, handbags and coffins be made from mushrooms?

Environment

To display this content from YouTube, you must enable advertisement tracking and audience measurement. Accept Manage my choices One of your browser extensions seems to be blocking the video player from loading. To watch this content, you may need to disable it on this site. Try again DOWN TO EARTH © FRANCE 24 03:35

03:35 min Share

From the show

Reading time 1 min

Can mushrooms replace leather and plastic? Thanks to mycelium derived from fungi, companies can now make 100 percent biodegradable products such as shoes and furniture that look exactly like leather. Major brands are already trying to make the shift. Our France 2 colleagues report.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the innovative use of mycelium from fungi to create 100% biodegradable products like shoes, furniture, surfboards, handbags, and coffins, which can replace leather and plastic. This represents a significant environmental breakthrough with broad impact, as major brands are adopting this sustainable alternative, potentially reducing pollution and waste on a large scale.
articles.personality_title: Mushrooms offer a new way to make biodegradable products like shoes and surfboards
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many products today, like shoes, handbags, and furniture, are made from leather or plastic. These materials can harm the environment because they take a long time to break down and create pollution.

**What happened** – Scientists and companies have started using mycelium, the root-like part of mushrooms, to create new materials. These mushroom-based materials look like leather but are completely biodegradable. Now, products such as shoes, surfboards, handbags, and even coffins can be made from this natural material. Big brands are already trying to use it.

**Impact** – This is important because it offers a natural alternative to plastic and leather, which usually take years to decompose. Using mycelium means these products can break down quickly without harming the planet. It could reduce waste and pollution from everyday items.

**What's next step** – As more companies test and adopt mushroom-based materials, we may see more products made from fungi in stores soon. This could encourage more research and help make these eco-friendly materials affordable and common.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Mushroom-based materials are becoming a real, eco-friendly option for making everyday products that normally harm the environment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les champignons, nouvelle matière biodégradable pour chaussures et planches de surf
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup de produits comme les chaussures, sacs à main ou meubles sont fabriqués en cuir ou en plastique, des matériaux qui polluent car ils mettent longtemps à se décomposer.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques et entreprises utilisent maintenant le mycélium, la partie racinaire des champignons, pour créer des matériaux qui ressemblent au cuir mais sont entièrement biodégradables. On peut fabriquer chaussures, planches de surf, sacs à main et même des cercueils avec ce matériau naturel. De grandes marques commencent à l’adopter.

**Impact** – C’est important car c’est une alternative naturelle au plastique et au cuir qui mettent des années à se décomposer. Le mycélium permet à ces produits de se dégrader rapidement sans polluer la planète, réduisant ainsi les déchets.

**Prochaine étape** – Plus d’entreprises vont tester et utiliser ces matériaux à base de champignons, ce qui pourrait rendre ces produits plus courants et abordables dans les magasins.

**Phrase clé** – Les matériaux à base de champignons deviennent une vraie option écologique pour fabriquer des objets du quotidien qui polluent habituellement.
articles.personality_title_es: Los champiñones, nueva materia biodegradable para zapatos y tablas de surf
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos productos como zapatos, bolsos y muebles se fabrican con cuero o plástico, materiales que contaminan porque tardan mucho en descomponerse.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos y empresas están usando micelio, la parte similar a raíces de los champiñones, para crear materiales que parecen cuero pero son 100 % biodegradables. Ahora se pueden hacer zapatos, tablas de surf, bolsos e incluso ataúdes con este material natural. Grandes marcas ya lo están intentando.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque ofrece una alternativa natural al plástico y cuero, que suelen tardar años en descomponerse. Usar micelio permite que estos productos se descompongan rápido sin dañar el planeta, ayudando a reducir basura y contaminación.

**Próximo paso** – A medida que más empresas prueben y usen materiales hechos con champiñones, podríamos ver más productos ecológicos en las tiendas pronto y que sean más accesibles.

**Frase clave** – Los materiales a base de champiñones se están convirtiendo en una opción ecológica real para fabricar productos cotidianos que normalmente contaminan.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a variety of eco-friendly products—a surfboard, a handbag, and a coffin—each subtly textured with the intricate, natural patterns of mushroom mycelium, set against a soft, earthy background of gentle browns and greens, symbolizing sustainable innovation and biodegradable materials.
